'''Solomon Haumono''' ({{langx|to|Solomone Haumono}}; born 13 October 1975) is an Australian former [[boxing|professional boxer]] and [[rugby league]] player.

==Early life==
He attended Newtown Boys High School, [[Newtown, New South Wales]]. He then went on to [[Christian Brothers' High School, Lewisham]] and represented them in the New South Wales Combined Catholic Colleges Australian Schoolboys team 1993.<ref>[http://www.sportingpulse.com/assoc_page.cgi?c=7-3444-0-0-0&sID=33364# www.sportingpulse.com] NSWCCC Players Representing Australian Schoolboys 1972–2007</ref>

==Rugby league career==
In [[rugby league]], his preferred position was {{rlp|sr}}. He played in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]] for the [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]], the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]], the [[Balmain Tigers]], the [[St George Illawarra Dragons]], and in the [[Super League]] for the [[London Broncos#1994–2005: Broncos and Super League|London Broncos]]/[[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins RL]]. Haumono played at representative level for [[New South Wales Rugby League team|New South Wales]] and [[Australian national rugby league team|Australia]], although these three state matches and one international match were in 1997, when Super League contracted players were ineligible for State of Origin selection. He also captained [[Tonga national rugby league team|Tonga]].{{citation needed|date=October 2024}}

Haumono made his first grade debut for Manly-Warringah in round 11, 1994 against [[Balmain Tigers|Balmain]], coming off the bench in a 42–0 victory at [[Leichhardt Oval]]. In 1995, Haumono featured heavily in the Manly side which won the minor premiership having lost only 2 matches all year. Haumono played from the bench in Manly's shock grand final loss to Canterbury. In 1996, Haumono played 14 games but missed out on selection in Manly's premiership winning side which defeated [[St. George Dragons|St. George]] in the grand final.

In 1997, Haumono signed with Canterbury who aligned themselves with the rival Super League competition during the [[Super League war]]. During the [[1998 NRL season]], Haumono walked out on Canterbury midway through the year after devising a plan to get out of his contract with the club. Haumono's plan was to join his supposed girlfriend [[Gabrielle Richens]] in England. Haumono elaborated on the plan saying "I was under contract for the Bulldogs so it was decided to come up with a plan that would ultimately force the Bulldogs to rip up my contract, So the plan was formed that I was going to chase after my girlfriend at the time in England. So off I went and before I knew it, the paper got a hold of it and blew it up, making headline news and myself being chased by the media at home and even there in London".<ref name=SMH1>{{cite web|url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/sol-dash-was-business-not-pleasure-20110819-1j279.html/|title=Dash was business not pleasure |work=The Sydney Morning Herald |date=20 August 2011 |access-date=18 January 2022}}</ref>

The plan was devised by Haumono and close friend [[Anthony Mundine]] but fell over when Canterbury got wind of the plot and instead fined Haumono and put him in reserve grade.<ref name=SMH1/>

In 1999, Haumono signed with [[Balmain Tigers|Balmain]]. Haumono only made 8 appearances for Balmain due to an elbow injury. In 2000, Haumono joined St George but only featured in 2 games, the last of which was the club's humiliating 70–10 loss against [[Melbourne Storm|Melbourne]] at the [[Melbourne Cricket Ground]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/05/01/st-george-illawarra-dragons-v-melbourne-storm-round-9-preview/|title=Dragons v Storm: Scott out, Olam and Robson to make debuts|website=NRL|date=May 2018 }}</ref>

===Return to League===
{{BLP unreferenced section|date=February 2025}}
Following a second spell with [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles|Manly]] in 2003, Haumono joined the [[London Broncos#1994–2005: Broncos and Super League|London Broncos]] and played in the capital for two seasons.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 November 2004 |title=Broncos sign Haumono |url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/broncos-sign-haumono-20041115-gdk47j.html |access-date=16 February 2025 |website=The Sydney Morning Herald |language=en}}</ref> In December 2006, with a year remaining on his contract, Haumono quit rugby league to return to professional boxing.

==Boxing career==
===2000 to 2002===
Haumono first took time out of rugby league after his spell with the [[St George Illawarra Dragons]] in 2000.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/boxing-haumonos-fight-not-biggest-he-has-faced/4GLTZFGKIDB2HVYX6CDCJJ45GM/ |title=Boxing: Haumono's fight not biggest he has faced |work=The New Zealand Herald |date=16 July 2016 |access-date=30 November 2021}}</ref> He followed in the footsteps of his father (Australian former heavyweight champion Maile Haumono) to take up a career in professional boxing. He fought eight times between 2000 and 2002, winning all of his [[heavyweight]] contests inside the distance.{{citation needed|date=October 2024}} Haumono briefly held the title of New South Wales heavyweight champion before rejecting an offer to join the stable of American promoter [[Don King (boxing promoter)|Don King]] and returning to rugby league.{{citation needed|date=October 2024}}

===Return to boxing – post 2008 ===
Haumono resumed his boxing career with a first-round knockout on 7 March 2007. He began training under trainer [[Johnny Lewis (boxing trainer)|Johnny Lewis]] who has assisted Haumono in reaching his 14–0 (14 KOs) record in the heavyweight ranks. He fought [[Cliff Couser]] on 27 August 2008 and won the fight by unanimous decision. He fought Colin Wilson on 11 March 2009 with the fight going to a draw after ten rounds. Solomon won his next fight by disqualification on 16 May 2009 against Royce Sio (1–0–0). Haumono was floored in the eleventh second of the first round by a right hook from Sio, but Sio then leaned down and hit Haumono again while Haumono's back was on the ground, leading to an instant disqualification. Haumono was back on his feet a few seconds later.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/more-sports/boxing/haumono-felled-by-brain-explosion/news-story/44e954c666746f9a61fbadd8872b12e2 |title=Solomon Haumono flattened, felled by more punches as he lay on the canvas |date=18 May 2009 |publisher=FoxSports |access-date=18 January 2022}}</ref>

Haumono fought [[Justin Whitehead]] on 16 August 2009 and lost for the first time in his career by split decision. He defeated Michael Kirby by tenth-round TKO for the OPBF Heavyweight Title on 18 September 2009, and became the Australian Heavyweight Champion after a tenth-round TKO of Franklin Egobi in Melbourne on 7 September 2012.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.stuff.co.nz/sport/8056216/Australian-champ-has-sound-advice-for-SBW |title=Australian champ has sound advice for SBW |author=Daniel Lane |publisher=Stuff |date=9 December 2012 |access-date=18 January 2022}}</ref>

On 31 December 2012, he fought [[K-1]] legend [[Kyotaro Fujimoto|Kyotaro Fuijmoto]] under boxing rules, defeating him in the fifth round via technical knockout.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/newslocal/parramatta/sport/heavyweight-champ-solomon-haumono-overcomes-japans-undefeated-kyotaro-fujimoto/news-story/f5acb787f3ad893847ed77322ae3b8a1|title=Heavyweight champ Solomon Haumono overcomes Japan's undefeated Kyotaro Fujimoto|publisher=The Daily Telegraph|accessdate=25 December 2025}}</ref>

On 28 April 2013, he lost by technical to [[Kevin Johnson (boxer)|Kevin Johnson]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.boxing247.com/boxing-news/lucas-browne-outpoints-james-toney-kevin-johnson-stops-solomon-haumono/11713|title=Lucas Browne Outpoints James Toney, Kevin Johnson Stops Solomon Haumono|publisher=Boxing News 24/7|accessdate=25 December 2025}}</ref>

On 21 July 2016, he lost by technical to [[Joseph Parker]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/boxing-joseph-parker-knocks-out-solomon-haumono-in-christchurch/7RTXWB4YFUNBAG3FXRHDG6OXNY/ |title=Boxing: Joseph Parker knocks out Solomon Haumono in Christchurch |work=The New Zealand Herald |date=21 July 2016 |access-date=30 November 2021}}</ref>

==Personal life==
Haumono is married to Margaret, who is a cousin of his friend and fellow rugby league player-turned boxer [[Anthony Mundine]]. Solomon and Margaret have five children.<ref>[https://www.couriermail.com.au/sport/solomon-huamono-knows-its-a-case-of-now-or-never-for-his-boxing-career/news-story/e439f4e71df97593da4d93585e7b8b4a?sv=b69963fa0dec52470afd5cbc975ccac6 "Solomon Huamono knows its a case of now or never for his boxing career"], by: Grantlee Kieza From: ''The Courier-Mail'' 7 March 2012 12:00 a.m.</ref>

He dated English model [[Gabrielle Richens]] for a period of time during his time with Canterbury.

In 2015, Haumono revealed he had been suffering a [[drug addiction]] during his league career. He was addicted to ice, cocaine and ecstasy.<ref>{{cite web |date=26 October 2015 |title=Drugs, guns and suicide: Solomon Haumono's explosive confessions of out-of-control addiction |url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/drugs-guns-and-suicide-solomon-haumonos-explosive-confessions-of-his-outofcontrol-addiction/news-story/d5778c03b654865a4f706bdfa94d0fac |access-date=30 November 2021 |work=The Daily Telegraph |location=London}}</ref>
